← World Gallery
Regions Artists Timeline All works

Europe

Post-Roman — illuminated manuscripts to post-impressionism.

A thousand years of churches, altarpieces, and — eventually — secular portraits, landscapes, and the individual human strangeness at the turn of the 20th century.

Click any image to enlarge. Use ← → or close with Esc.

500 – 1000

Icon of the Virgin and Child
Egypt, Byzantine period · 500s CE · wool and dye
12 Nummi (Coin) of a Byzantine Emperor
by Byzantine, minted in Alexandria
Byzantine Empire · byzantine · 500–599 · Bronze
Pilgrimage Jug with Christian Symbols
by Byzantine; Jerusalem
Latin Kingdom of Jerusalem · byzantine · 578–636 · Glass
Solidus (Coin) of Heraclius
by Byzantine; minted in Constantinople
Byzantine Empire · byzantine · 638–641 · Gold
Solidus (Coin) of Empress Irene
by Byzantine; minted in Constantinople
Istanbul · byzantine · 797–802 · Gold
Book of Kells
Insular Celtic · c. 800 CE · Ireland
A Latin gospel book from monks on Iona and Kells. The ornament is so intricate that Gerald of Wales thought it "the work of an angel, not of a man."
Solidus (Coin) of Theophilus
by Byzantine, minted in Constantinople
Byzantine Empire · byzantine · 829–831 · Gold
Byzantine Mosaics of Hagia Sophia
Byzantine · 867 CE onward · Constantinople
Figural mosaics on gold ground. The Virgin and Child in the apse, unveiled in 867 to reverse the Iconoclasm, glows from half a kilometer away.
Solidus (Coin) of Basil I with Christ Enthroned
by Byzantine, minted in Constantinople
Byzantine Empire · byzantine · 868–870 · Gold
Anonymous Follis (Coin)
by Byzantine
Istanbul · byzantine · 976–1028 · Bronze

1000 – 1500

Histamenon (Coin) of Romanus III Argyrus with Christ Enthroned
by Byzantine; minted in Constantinople
Istanbul · byzantine · 1028–1034 · Gold
Saint Sophia Cathedral, Kyiv
Byzantine / Kyivan Rus · 1037 · Ukraine
A Byzantine five-nave cathedral at the heart of Kyivan Rus. Its mosaics of the Virgin Orans ("Indestructible Wall") are said to have protected the city through a thousand years of war.
Bayeux Tapestry
Norman · c. 1070 · France
Seventy meters of embroidered linen telling the Norman conquest of England. Probably stitched by English nuns for the new conqueror's half-brother.
Pendant Icon with the Virgin "Dexiokratousa"
Byzantium, Byzantine period, 12th century · 1100s · steatite, gilt silver, pearls
Pendant Icon with the Virgin "Dexiokratousa" and Frame with Winged Bull of Saint Luke on the back
Byzantium (pendant); Germany, Aachen (frame), Byzantine period, 12th century (pendant); Byzantine period, 14th century (frame) · pendant 1100s; frame mid-1300s · steatite, gilt silver, pearls
Fragment of an Icon with Scenes from Christ's Passion
Byzantium, Byzantine period, 12th century · 1100s · steatite
Pala d'Oro
Byzantine → Gothic goldwork · 976–1345 · Venice
The golden altarpiece of San Marco. 250 enameled panels, 1,927 gems, looted from Constantinople in 1204 and then added to across four centuries. Still in daily use.
Santiago de Compostela Cathedral
Romanesque · 1075–1211 · Galicia
The end of the Camino de Santiago — pilgrims walk 800 kilometers to this high altar and the silver tomb of St. James. The Botafumeiro swings eighty feet across the transept at mass.
Saint Gregory as Author
by Scriptorium of Weingarten Abbey
Germany · medieval · 1188–1200 · Opaque watercolor on parchment
Lion Aquamanile
Germany, Lower Saxony, Hildesheim, Gothic period, first half 13th century · 1200–1250 · bronze: cast, chased, and punched
Head of an Apostle
by French; Paris
France · medieval · 1200–1220 · Limestone
Chartres Cathedral
High Gothic · 1194–1220 · France
Rebuilt in 26 years after a fire. Its stained glass — especially the "Chartres blue" — has never been matched; the recipe was lost.
Head of an Apostle from the South Portal of the Cathedral of Thérouanne
Northern France (Pas-de-Calais), Thérouanne, 13th century · c. 1235–1240 · oolitic limestone
Westminster Abbey
English Gothic · 1245 onward · London
Coronation church of English monarchs since 1066; also a vast, crowded graveyard — Newton, Darwin, Stephen Hawking. Poets' Corner adds Chaucer, Dickens, T. S. Eliot.
Reims Cathedral
High Gothic · 1211–1275 · France
Coronation church of the kings of France. Shelled for five years in World War I; its sculptures, including the famous Smiling Angel, shattered and then were painstakingly reassembled.
Cologne Cathedral
High Gothic · 1248–1880 · Germany
Started in 1248, stopped in 1473, resumed in 1842, finished with the original medieval drawings in hand. For four years after completion it was the tallest building on earth.
Siena Cathedral
Italian Gothic · 1196–1348
Striped in black and white marble. The floor is inlaid with 56 narrative panels — the largest program of marble intarsia in Italy, covered most of the year to protect it.
Notre-Dame de Paris
Gothic · 1163–1345 · Paris
Cathedral of the kings of France. Half the fabric is original medieval, half is Viollet-le-Duc's 19th-century reimagining. The 2019 fire took the spire.
Gothic Bible (Vulgate)
Southern France, Toulouse or Avignon, 13th century · c. 1275–1300 · ink, tempera, and gold on vellum
Table of Bigamy, from the Summa Super Titulis Decretalium Completa
by French
England · medieval · 1275–1299 · Manuscript cutting in tempera and gold leaf
Rose Window
Gothic · 13th c. · Strasbourg
A massive tracery wheel of stained glass — the Gothic cathedral's visual sermon, laying out salvation in colored light. Chartres' rose blue was never surpassed; Strasbourg's west rose is a rival.
Bifolium from a Bible: Initial E[t factum est] with Ezekiel
France, Paris · c. 1290 · ink, tempera, and gold on vellum
Bishop at Mass in a Historiated Initial "P" from a Choirbook
by French or possibly southern Netherlandish
France · medieval · 1290–1310 · Manuscript cutting in tempera and gold leaf
Miniatures from a Manuscript of the Apocalypse
France, Lorraine, 13th century · c. 1295 · ink, tempera, and gold on vellum
Praying Saint (Moses?) with Christ in a Historiated Initial "L", from a Choirbook
by Italian
Italy · medieval · 1290–1310 · Manuscript cutting in tempera and gold leaf
Florence Cathedral (Duomo)
Italian Gothic / Renaissance · 1296–1436
Brunelleschi's dome, still the largest brick dome ever built. He designed it without drawings, built it without scaffolding, and took the method with him to the grave.
Madonna and Child
by Italian
Italy · 14th century · 1300–1400 · Marble
Fragment of an Icon of the Crucifixion with Mary Magdalene and the Virgin Mary
Byzantium, Byzantine period, 14th century · 1300s · steatite
Scrovegni Chapel Frescoes
by Giotto di Bondone
Proto-Renaissance · 1303–1305 · Padua
Giotto's cycle of the life of the Virgin and Christ. A man paid for it to save his father from hell — his father appears in the painting, handing over the chapel model.
Processional Cross
by Master of Santa Chiara
Italy, Umbria · c. 1320 · oil on canvas on wood
Table Fountain
France, Paris · c. 1320–40 · gilt-silver and translucent enamels
Frame with Winged Bull of Saint Luke on the back
Germany, Aachen, Byzantine period, 14th century · mid-1300s · gilt silver
Diptych with Scenes from the Life of Christ
by French; Paris
France · 14th century · 1340–1360 · Ivory

France, Champagne · c. 1350 · alabaster with traces of paint and gilding

by German; Rhenish
Germany · 14th century · 1360–1380 · Stone
Saint John the Baptist

France · Gothic (medieval) · 1370–1380 · Alabaster

Fragment
by Italy
Italy · Renaissance · 1401–1425 · Silk and gilt-animal-substrate-wrapped linen
Panel from a Cassone: The Race of the Palio in the Streets of Florence
by Giovanni Francesco Toscani
Italy, Florence, 15th century · 1418 · tempera and gold on wood
Icon of the Mother of God and Infant Christ (Virgin Eleousa)
by Angelos Akotantos
Republic of Venice, Kingdom of Candia, Candia (Heraklion, Crete, Greece) or Byzantine Empire, Constantinople (Istanbul, Turkey) · c. 1425–50 · tempera and gold on wood
Ghent Altarpiece
by Jan van Eyck
Early Netherlandish · Jan van Eyck · 1432 · Belgium
Twelve folding panels of unmatched detail. Stolen seven times across its history; the Just Judges panel has been missing since 1934.
The Arnolfini Portrait
by Jan van Eyck
Early Netherlandish · Jan van Eyck · 1434
A merchant and his wife in a Bruges interior. The convex mirror on the back wall reflects two more figures — one of them possibly the painter.
Portrait of Leonello D'Este, Marquess of Ferrara (obverse and reverse)
by Pisanello
Italy, 15th century · c. 1440–1444 · bronze
St. Catherine of Siena and the Beggar

Italy, Siena · 1460s · tempera and gold on wood

Virgin and Child with an Angel
by Sandro Botticelli
Italy · Renaissance · 1475–1485 · Tempera on panel
Panels from the High Altar of the Charterhouse of Saint-Honoré, Thuison-les-Abbeville: The Last Supper
by French; Picardy

Portrait of a Man in a Red Cap
by South German; possibly Ulm
Germany · 15th century · 1475–1485 · Oil on panel
The Last Judgment
by Alart du Hameel
Netherlands · 1478–1506 · Engraving in black on buff laid paper
Primavera
by Sandro Botticelli
Early Renaissance · Botticelli · c. 1480 · Florence
Venus in a grove of citrus. The three Graces, Mercury, and the story of Chloris turning into Flora — a Medici wedding gift, decoded differently in every generation.
The Adoration of the Magi
by Geertgen tot Sint Jans
Early Netherlandish · c. 1480
Mary and the child Jesus in a rubble-walled ruin, the magi advancing in procession across the background. Geertgen's pale, doll-like figures and closed compositions influenced Dutch painting for a century.
St. John with Serpent in Chalice
by Israhel van Meckenem
Germany · c. 1480–1500 · engraving
Diptych of Saints Achatius, Barbara, Apollonia, and Sebald
by German; Franconia
Germany · 15th century · 1480–1500 · Distemper on linen
The Birth of Venus
by Sandro Botticelli
Early Renaissance · Botticelli · c. 1485 · Florence
Venus rises from the sea on a shell, blown to shore by wind-gods. Painted on canvas when fresco was still the fashion — and kept hidden from Savonarola's fires.
Virgin and Child with the Young Saint John the Baptist
by Sandro Botticelli
Italy, 15th century · c. 1490 · tempera and oil on wood

Germany, late 15th Century-early 16th Century · c. 1498 · engraving
Drawings by Leonardo da Vinci
by Leonardo da Vinci
c. 1480–1510 · Chalk, ink, and silverpoint on paper
The Louvre holds over twenty sheets by Leonardo — studies of hands, horses, the tilt of a woman's head, the flow of water. A single silverpoint line, set down and never corrected.
Venus with a Burning Urn
Northern Italy, 16th century · c. 1500–1520 · gilt bronze
The Garden of Paradise
by Workshop of Hieronymus Bosch
Netherlands · 16th Century · 1495–1525 · Oil on panel
Archers Shooting at a Herm, Triumph of Bacchus, and Other Studies
by after Michelangelo Buonarroti
Italy · 1495–1564 · Pen and brown ink
Head of an Old Woman
by Hans Holbein
Germany, late 15th-early 16th Century · c. 1500 · silverpoint; traces of framing lines in graphite
A Lady with Three Suitors
France, 16th century · c. 1500 · pen and brown ink, brush and brown wash, with traces of black chalk
Entombment
by German; possibly Cologne
Germany · 16th Century · 1495–1505 · Tempera on panel
The Last Supper
by Leonardo da Vinci
High Renaissance · Leonardo · 1495–1498 · Milan
Painted on a refectory wall in an experimental technique that began flaking within decades. The fragility is part of the legend.
The Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse, from The Apocalypse
by Albrecht Dürer
Germany · 1496–1498 · Woodcut in black on ivory laid paper
St Jerome in Penitence
by Dürer, Albrecht
Nuremberg · ca 1496
Saint Eustace
by Albrecht Dürer
Germany, late 15th-early 16th Century · c. 1501 · engraving
The Monstrous Sow of Landser
by Albrecht Dürer
Germany · 1496 · Engraving in black on ivory laid paper
Pietà
by Michelangelo
High Renaissance · Michelangelo · 1498–1499 · Vatican
Carved by a 24-year-old. Mary is impossibly young — younger than her son — because, Michelangelo said, virtue preserves her. His only signed work.
The Lamentation, from The Large Passion
by Albrecht Dürer
Germany · 1498–1499 · Woodcut in black on ivory laid paper

1500 – 1800

The Garden of Earthly Delights
by Hieronymus Bosch
Early Netherlandish · Bosch · c. 1500 · Spain
A triptych of Eden, excess, and hell. The right panel is crowded with monsters nobody has fully decoded — including one with sheet music tattooed on a buttock.
Adoration of the Magi
by Titian
Italy, 16th century · 1500s · oil on canvas
Casket
Italy · ca. 1500
Joanna of Aragon
by After Raffaello Sanzio, called Raphael
Italy · 1500–1520 · Black chalk with red chalk on ivory laid paper
Statuette pacing horse
by Antico (Pier Jacopo di Antonio Alari-Bonacolsi)
Italy · 1500-1550
Father Time
by Italian
Italy · sixteenth century · 1500–1600 · Bronze
Three Ships at Sea
by Possibly School of Pieter Bruegel, the elder
Netherlands · 1500–1700 · Pen and brown ink on ivory laid paper

by Marcantonio Raimondi
Italy, 16th century · c. 1505 · engraving
Panel
by Italy
Italy · Renaissance · 1501–1600 · Silk and linen
"Renaissance" Fringe
by Italy
Italy · Renaissance · 1501–1600 · Silk and gold and silver gilt strips wound around silk fiber cores
The Stag Hunt
by Lucas Cranach the Elder
Germany · 1501–1511 · Woodcut in black from two blocks on two sheets of cream laid paper
David
by Michelangelo
High Renaissance · Michelangelo · 1501–1504
Seventeen feet of marble carved from a block others had given up on. David faces Goliath before the stone flies — tense, not triumphant.
Saint Lawrence
by Tilman Riemenschneider

The School of Athens
by Raphael
High Renaissance · Raphael · 1509–1511
Plato and Aristotle at the center, surrounded by the Greek philosophical tradition. Raphael slipped portraits of himself and Michelangelo into the crowd.
Sistine Chapel Ceiling
by Michelangelo
High Renaissance · Michelangelo · 1508–1512
Four years, mostly alone, on scaffolding. Michelangelo considered himself a sculptor, not a painter, and complained about the job the whole way through.
Mona Lisa (La Joconde)
by Leonardo da Vinci
c. 1503–1519 · Oil on poplar · Salle des États
Probably a portrait of Lisa Gherardini, wife of a Florentine merchant. Leonardo carried it with him for years, reworking it endlessly, and it never left his side until his death in France. Her fame owes as much to theft as to genius: when Vincenzo Peruggia stole her in 1911 and kept her in a Paris apartment for two years, the empty wall drew larger crowds than the painting ever had.
The Life of the Virgin
by Albrecht Dürer
Germany, early 16th Century · c. 1510–11 · woodcut
Copy of Raphael's Massacre of the Innocents

The Harrowing of Hell - Christ in Limbo, plate nine from The Large Passion

Time (from Chateau de Chaumont set)
France, Lyon(?), early 16th century · 1512–15 · silk and wool; tapestry weave
The Triumph of Eternity (from Chateau de Chaumont set)
France, Lyon(?), early 16th century · 1512–15 · silk and wool; tapestry weave
Youth (from Chateau de Chaumont set)
France, Lyon(?), early 16th century · 1512–15 · silk and wool; tapestry weave
Chateau de Chaumont Set
France, Lyon(?), early 16th century · 1512–1515 · silk and wool; tapestry weave
Knight, Death and the Devil
by Albrecht Dürer
1513 · Engraving
One of Dürer's three "Master Engravings." A Christian knight rides impassively through a desolate gorge, unmoved by the hourglass-bearing Death beside him or the goat-headed Devil behind. The technical summit of Renaissance printmaking.
Sistine Madonna
by Raphael
High Renaissance · 1512–1513 · Dresden
Mary descending on clouds; two bored cherubs resting their chins on the painting's lower frame. Those cherubs — cropped, enlarged, spun off — became one of the most reproduced images of any era.
Descent from the Cross
by Ugo da Carpi
Italy, 16th century · c. 1518 · chiaroscuro woodcut
Knight, Death, and the Devil
by Albrecht Dürer
Germany · 1513 · engraving
The Isenheim Altarpiece
by Matthias Grünewald
Northern Renaissance · Grünewald · 1512–1516 · Alsace
Painted for a hospital for plague and ergotism. Christ's body on the cross is covered in exactly the sores the patients saw on their own.
Melencolia I
by Albrecht Dürer
Northern Renaissance · 1514
A winged figure sits surrounded by tools — compass, scales, hourglass — unable to act. Dürer's engraving of creative despair, loaded with symbols art historians still argue about.
The Dying Slave
by Michelangelo
c. 1513–1515 · Marble · Denon Wing
Intended for the tomb of Pope Julius II, a project that consumed Michelangelo for forty years and was never finished as planned. The figure is not dying but — perhaps — falling asleep, or surrendering to ecstasy. Michelangelo left the back deliberately rough.
St Jerome in his Study
by Dürer, Albrecht
Nuremberg · 1514
The Submersion of Pharaoh's Army in the Red Sea

Italy, Venice · 1514–15, printed 1549 · woodcut

The Adoration of the Magi (Mostaert)
by Jan Mostaert
Early Netherlandish · c. 1520
Mostaert paints the kings and attendants in crisp Flemish costume against a fantasy architecture, a Haarlem master still working from the Van Eyck playbook a century later.

Dance around the Golden Calf
by Lucas van Leyden
Northern Renaissance · c. 1530
While Moses is up the mountain, the Israelites feast and dance around the idol — and Lucas van Leyden stages it as a Flemish beer garden. Religious lesson delivered as raucous genre scene.

by Follower of Leonardo da Vinci
Italy · 1530–1540 · Pen and brown ink on tan laid paper
The Ambassadors
by Hans Holbein the Younger
Northern Renaissance · 1533
Two French diplomats stand beside a table of instruments — lutes, globes, sundials. At the bottom, a distorted smear that becomes a human skull when you look from the side. A memento mori in code.

Italy · 1548–1552 · Black chalk heightened with white chalk
Samson Destroying the Temple of the Philistines
by Maarten van Heemskerck
Northern Mannerism · 1550
Samson pulls the pillars as Philistine nobles fall in an architectural eruption. Heemskerck learned to twist bodies in Rome under Michelangelo's shadow; he brought that torsion home to Haarlem.
The Blind Leading the Blind
by Pieter van der Heyden
Netherlands, 16th century · c. 1561 · engraving

by After Michelangelo Buonarroti

Ascending Male Nude
by After Michelangelo Buonarroti
Italy · 1550–1559 · Black chalk on tan laid paper
The Egg Dance
by Pieter Aertsen
Northern Renaissance · 1552
In a crowded tavern, a young man dances around an egg without breaking it — a Flemish game with obvious symbolism. Pieter Aertsen paints peasant life as crowded, alive, and morally ambiguous.

by Lucas Cranach, the Younger
Germany · 1561 · Woodcut in black with letterpress on ivory laid paper
The Wedding at Cana
by Paolo Veronese
Venetian Renaissance · 1563 · Louvre, Paris
The largest painting in the Louvre at seventy square meters. Painted for a Venetian refectory; Napoleon's troops cut it in half in 1797 to ship it to France.
The Tower of Babel
by Pieter Bruegel the Elder
Northern Renaissance · 1563
Bruegel's spiralling ziggurat in a Flemish landscape — already cracking, already doomed. A single tiny king-and-retinue at the base tells you who ordered the hubris.
Hunters in the Snow
by Pieter Bruegel the Elder
Northern Renaissance · 1565
Three hunters returning empty-handed to a frozen village; dogs slink behind them; skaters on the far ponds. One of the most lived-in winter scenes ever painted.
Design (plan and elevation) for a papal monument
by Sormani, Leonardo
Italy · ca. 1565-1582
The Hare Hunters
by Pieter Bruegel, the elder

by Giorgio Vasari
Italy, 16th century · 1566–68 · Black chalk, pen and brown ink, and extensive white heightening (wash and point of brush), with incising on paper prepared with a brown wash.

by Italian
Italy · sixteenth century · 1590–1610 · Bronze
The Holy Family with Mary Magdalen
by El Greco
Spain · c. 1590–95 · oil on canvas
Vertumnus
by Giuseppe Arcimboldo
Mannerism · 1591
Portrait of Emperor Rudolf II as Vertumnus, god of seasons — his face built from pears, grapes, squash, grain. Arcimboldo's joke on Habsburg dignity; the emperor loved it.
Standing Cup
London, England: late 16th century, late 16th century · 1593 · silver, partially gilt
Standing Cup and Cover
London, England, late 16th century · 1593 · silver, partially gilt
Cover for a Standing Cup
London, England, late 16th century · 1593 · silver, partially gilt
The Cardsharps
by Caravaggio
Baroque · c. 1594
An early Caravaggio: a boy is cheated at cards by a pair of accomplices — one palming a card behind his back, the other reading his hand over his shoulder. A career-making genre scene.
Allegory of Venus and Cupid
by Imitator of Titian
Italy · 17th Century · 1595–1605 · Oil on canvas
Venus after the Bath
by Giambologna
Italy, early 17th century · c. 1600 · bronze
Bacchus
by Caravaggio
Baroque · c. 1596 · Florence
Caravaggio's boyish, tipsy Bacchus — fingernails dirty, a decaying fruit basket in the foreground. Lost for three centuries, rediscovered in a Uffizi storeroom in 1913.
Henry VIII, Elizabeth I, and Edward VI
by English
England · 16th Century · 1597 · Oil on panel
A Village Kermis
by Savery, Jacob
Southern Netherlands · 1598
The Calling of Saint Matthew
by Caravaggio
Baroque · 1599–1600 · Rome
Christ points across a shadowed tavern. A shaft of light slides down the back wall. Matthew gestures at himself — "me?" — caravaggio inventing a whole new visual language.
Judith Beheading Holofernes
by Caravaggio
Baroque · c. 1599
Judith, arms extended to keep the blood off her dress, saws through the Assyrian general's neck. Caravaggio's first great biblical violence — the beginning of his reputation.
St. Peter's Basilica
Renaissance &amp; Baroque · 1506–1626 · Vatican
Built over the tomb of Peter. Bramante started it, Michelangelo finished the dome, Bernini added the piazza's embracing colonnade — the collective monument of Renaissance Rome.
Guard Drawing His Sword
by After Raffaello Sanzio, called Raphael

Bacchus (recto); Architectural Sketches (verso)
by after Michelangelo Buonarroti
Italy · 1600–1699 · Black chalk and graphite
Sir Thomas More
by Hans Holbein the Younger
Germany · 1600s · oil on wood in a gilt wood frame
The Ecstasy of Saint Francis
by Giovanni Baglione
Italy · 17th Century · 1601 · Oil on canvas
Samson and the Lion
by Cristoforo Stati
Italy · 17th Century · 1604–1607 · Marble
The Crucifixion of Saint Andrew
by Caravaggio
Italy, Naples · 1606–7 · oil on canvas
Flowers in a Glass
by Ambrosius Bosschaert
Netherlands · 1606 · oil on copper
The Petitions [right part] from The Story of Artemisia
by After a design by Antoine Caron
France · 1607–1630 · Wool and silk
Winter Landscape with Skaters
by Hendrick Avercamp
Dutch Golden Age · c. 1608
A frozen river crowded with skaters, golfers, fallen figures, horse-drawn sleds, and one distinctly unhelpful dog. Avercamp, deaf and mute, became the specialist of the Little Ice Age tondo.
Dying Adonis
by Hendrick Goltzius
Northern Mannerism · 1609
Adonis collapses from the boar's wound, Venus grieving over him — Goltzius's only surviving painting in the Rijksmuseum, from a printmaker who spent most of his career with a burin.
Samson and Delilah (Rubens)
by Peter Paul Rubens
Peter Paul Rubens · 1610 · National Gallery, London
Delilah watches tenderly while a Philistine crops Samson's hair as he sleeps on her lap. The candlelit nocturne and coiled composition mark Rubens's first mature statement after returning from Italy, still vibrating with Caravaggio.
A Castle, plate 22 after Pictures of Farms, Country Houses and Rustic Villages (Praediorum villarum et rusticarum casularum icones)
by Claes Jansz. Visscher
Netherlands · 1612 · Etching in black on ivory laid paper
Village Road, plate four after Pictures of Farms, Country Houses and Rustic Villages (Praediorum villarum et rusticarum casularum icones)
by Claes Jansz. Visscher
Netherlands · 1612 · Etching in black on ivory laid paper
Village Road, plate three after Pictures of Farms, Country Houses and Rustic Villages (Praediorum villarum et rusticarum casularum icones)
by Claes Jansz. Visscher
Netherlands · 1612 · Etching in black on ivory laid paper
Title Page, after Pictures of Farms, Country Houses and Rustic Villages (Praediorum villarum et rusticarum casularum icones)
by Claes Jansz. Visscher
Netherlands · 1612 · Etching in black on ivory laid paper
Portrait of a Man
by Tanzio da Varallo
Italy, 17th century · c. 1620 · oil on canvas
De Poolse Muts
by Balthasar van der Ast
Netherlands · c. 1620–30 · brush and watercolor in red, green, and yellow with white heightening or grey wash on antique laid cream paper
Samson and Delilah
by Gerrit van Honthorst
Netherlands · c. 1616 · oil on canvas
The Seven Archangels (recto); Studies of an Archangel and a Wind God (verso)
by Massimo Stanzione
Italy · c. 1620s · Pen and brown ink, brown wash, traces of black chalk (recto); pen and brown ink (verso),
The Seven Archangels (recto)
by Massimo Stanzione
Italy · c. 1620s · Pen and brown ink, brown wash, traces of black chalk (recto)
Studies of an Archangel and a Wind God (verso)
by Massimo Stanzione
Italy · c. 1620s · pen and brown ink (verso),
Danaë
by Orazio Gentileschi
Italy, 17th century · c. 1623 · oil on canvas
Kitchen Scene
by Diego Velázquez
Spain · 17th Century · 1618–1622 · Oil on canvas
Flower Still Life
by Ambrosius Bosschaert
Dutch Golden Age · 1619
An impossible bouquet — tulip, rose, iris, forget-me-not — all blooming simultaneously, in a Chinese Wan-Li vase. Bosschaert painted flowers that could not coexist in real time; a compressed catalogue of the seasons.
The Resurrection
by Cecco del Caravaggio
Italy · 17th Century · 1619–1620 · Oil on canvas
Portrait of a Boy
by School of Diego Velázquez
Spain · 1619–1700 · Black chalk
Village on a River
by Jan Jansz den Uyl
Netherlands · 1620–25 · black chalk, with transparent and opaque watercolor in blue, green-blue, gray, and ochre, framing line in brown ink on antique laid paper; framing line in brown ink
The Rape of Proserpina
by Gian Lorenzo Bernini
Gian Lorenzo Bernini · 1622 · Galleria Borghese
Pluto's fingertips sink visibly into Proserpina's thigh — marble impersonating flesh. Bernini was twenty-three; the group stunned Rome into realizing that Baroque sculpture had outrun the limits of Hellenistic virtuosity.
Isaac Massa and Beatrix van der Laen
by Frans Hals
Dutch Golden Age · 1622
A rare double marriage portrait set outdoors — husband's hand over his heart, wife smiling, a garden of love behind them. Breaking with every stiff Dutch betrothal convention before it.
The Merry Fiddler
by Gerard van Honthorst
Utrecht Caravaggism · 1623
A fiddler leans out of a window frame, raising his glass to the viewer — a character study made a picture. Honthorst brought Caravaggio's candle-lit night scenes home to the Netherlands.
Rest on the Flight into Egypt
by Guercino
Italy, 17th century · 1624 · oil on canvas
Apollo and Daphne
by Gian Lorenzo Bernini
Gian Lorenzo Bernini · 1625 · Galleria Borghese
Bernini caught the instant Daphne's fingers sprout leaves and her feet root into bark, her scream frozen just as Apollo's hand grazes her ribcage. He was twenty-six when he finished it and already the most technically audacious sculptor in Rome.
A Young Man with a Chain
by Rembrandt van Rijn
Netherlands · c. 1629 or 1632 · oil on wood
Interior of a Room with Figures: A Man Playing a Lute and a Woman
by Brouwer, Adriaen
Belgium · ca. 1625
The Rommel-Pot Player
by Workshop of Frans Hals
Netherlands · 17th Century · 1625–1635 · Oil on panel
Lot and His Daughters
by Jan Georg van Vliet
Netherlands · 1631 · etching and engraving
Still Life with a Turkey Pie
by Pieter Claesz
Dutch Golden Age · 1627
A gold-crusted pie topped with a stuffed turkey in full plumage — an absurd, showy centerpiece for a wealthy Haarlem household. Pieter Claesz's banketje genre at its most ostentatious.
Portrait of Philip IV
by Workshop of Diego Velázquez
Spain · 17th Century · 1627–1637 · Oil on canvas
Isabella of Bourbon, Wife of Philip IV of Spain
by Workshop of Diego Velázquez
Spain · 17th Century · 1627–1637 · Oil on canvas
The Merry Drinker
by Frans Hals
Dutch Golden Age · c. 1628
A cavalry officer catches the viewer's eye mid-toast, his wineglass still raised. Hals's loose wet-on-wet brushwork — visible in the stubble, the ruff, the sudden twinkle — set the template for Manet.
The Shepherdess
by Jacob Gerritsz Cuyp
Dutch Golden Age · 1628
A shepherdess seated with her crook and lamb in a rocky landscape — a quiet image by Aelbert Cuyp's father, a portrait-painter branching briefly into pastoral.
Two Children with a Cat
by Judith Leyster
Dutch Golden Age · 1630
A grinning boy clutches a reluctant cat while his sister teases its ear with a ribboned eel. One of the earliest double child portraits in Dutch painting, and Leyster's warmest surviving work.
Portrait of a Woman
by Rembrandt van Rijn
Netherlands · 1635 or earlier · oil on wood
Bust of a Youth (Saint John the Baptist)
by Francesco Mochi
Italy · Baroque · 1630–1640 · Marble on variegated black marble socle
Portrait of the Jester Calabazas
by Diego Velázquez
Spain · c. 1631–32 · oil on canvas
The Anatomy Lesson of Dr. Nicolaes Tulp
by Rembrandt
Dutch Golden Age · 1632
A public dissection of an executed criminal, witnessed by seven guild surgeons. Rembrandt was 26; it made his reputation.
The Garden of Love
by Peter Paul Rubens
Peter Paul Rubens · 1633 · Museo del Prado
A riotous outdoor party Rubens painted after his second marriage — his young wife Hélène Fourment appears three times among the guests. Cupids tug couples together while a fountain with the Three Graces soaks the scene in divinely sanctioned pleasure.
Study for the Head of St. Michael
by Pietro da Cortona
Italy, 17th century · 1633 · black chalk
Portrait of Tieleman Roosterman
by Frans Hals
Netherlands · 1634 · oil on canvas
Kitchen Still Life
by Attributed to Paolo Antonio Barbieri
Italy · 17th Century · 1635–1645 · Oil on canvas
Interior of the Church of St Bavo in Haarlem
by Pieter Saenredam
Dutch Golden Age · 1636
Saenredam surveyed the church meticulously, then painted it whiter, taller, more abstract than the thing itself. A Calvinist church emptied of images — and yet somehow this picture is still, and reverent.
Self portrait with Saskia
by Rembrandt van Rijn
Netherlands · 1636
Rembrandt and His Wife Saskia
by Rembrandt van Rijn
Netherlands · 1636 · etching
Self portrait with his wife Saskia
by Rembrandt van Rijn
Netherlands · 1636
The Cheat with the Ace of Diamonds
by Georges de La Tour
c. 1636–1638 · Oil on canvas · Sully Wing
Four figures, four glances, one con. A naïve young nobleman is about to lose his fortune to the cheat on the left, who has pulled an ace from his belt. The courtesan and her servant are in on it; only the young man isn't looking at anyone.
Self-Portrait as the Allegory of Painting
by Artemisia Gentileschi
Baroque · c. 1638–1639
Artemisia paints herself in the act of painting — arms muscled, sleeves pushed up. She was the first woman admitted to the Accademia di Belle Arti di Firenze.
Self portrait in a velvet cap with plume
by Rembrandt van Rijn
Netherlands · 1638
Portrait of a Woman, probably Aeltje Dircksdr. Pater
by Frans Hals
Netherlands · 1638 · oil on canvas
Self portrait leaning on a stone sill
by Rembrandt van Rijn
Netherlands · 1639
Pan Punished by Nymphs
by Jacques Jordaens
Flemish Baroque · c. 1640
Pan caught spying on sleeping nymphs — now held down and spanked with a fistful of reeds. Jordaens's peasant-bodied mythology, equal parts Ovid and tavern humor.
Tobias Healing His Father's Blindness
by Rembrandt van Rijn
Netherlands · c. 1640–45 · Pen and brown ink, touched with white gouache; framing lines in pen and brown ink
Scenes of Witchcraft: Night
by Salvator Rosa
Italy, 17th century · c. 1645–1649 · oil on canvas
Scenes of Witchcraft: Evening
by Salvator Rosa
Italy, 17th century · c. 1645–49 · oil on canvas
Scenes of Witchcraft: Day
by Salvator Rosa
Italy, 17th century · c. 1645–1649 · oil on canvas
Two Monks in a Landscape
by Italian or Spanish
Italy · 17th Century · 1640–1650 · Oil on canvas
Pax
England · ca. 1640
Portrait of a Girl Dressed in Blue
by Johannes Verspronck
Dutch Golden Age · 1641
A ten-year-old in a stiff satin dress, fan in hand, eyes fixed calmly on the viewer. Verspronck made one of the most-loved child portraits of the era — the girl herself is not identified.
Christ Taken before Caiaphas
by Rembrandt van Rijn
Netherlands · c. 1641–42 · pen and brown ink and brush and brown wash, with white gouache
The Monk in the Cornfield
by Rembrandt van Rijn
Netherlands · 1641–1651 · Etching with drypoint on ivory laid paper
The Night Watch
by Rembrandt
Dutch Golden Age · Rembrandt · 1642
A militia portrait transformed into a theatrical scene — figures stepping forward, a drumbeat, a girl glowing in the crowd. Not actually set at night.
Study of a Head seen from Below (verso)
by Francisque Millet
France, 17th century · c. 1650–75 · brush and gray and red wash
Faggot Gatherer (recto) Study of a Head seen from Below (verso)
by Francisque Millet
France, 17th century · third quarter 1600s · pen and brown ink and brush and gray and brown wash
Panoramic View of the Spaarne
by Jan van Goyen
Dutch Golden Age · 1644
An enormous sky and a low horizon — Haarlem on the right, a windmill on the left, the flat world between them. The template for every Dutch landscape that came after.
Castle on a River
by Salomon van Ruysdael
Netherlands · 1644 · oil on wood
River View near Deventer
by Salomon van Ruysdael
Dutch Golden Age · 1645
A ferry crosses a river under a milky sky, Deventer's silhouette on the far bank. Salomon van Ruysdael made dozens of riverine tonal landscapes — all similar, all good, like a slow weather report of the Republic.
Goat Lying Down
by Jan Baptist Weenix
Dutch Italianate · 1645
A tethered goat at the edge of an Italian ruin, painted with more tenderness than most portraits got that decade. Weenix is the Dutch painter who found holiness in livestock.
The Little Bridge
by Jacob van Ruisdael
Netherlands · c. 1650 · etching
Two Peasants and their Dog
by Jacob van Ruisdael
Netherlands · c. 1650 · etching
Refreshing the Thirsty
by Michael Sweerts
Flemish Baroque · 1646
From Sweerts's Seven Acts of Mercy series — a woman hands a cup to a kneeling man in the street. Painted in Rome, in a palette of clay and ash; his silent dignity is closer to Le Nain than to any Dutch contemporary.
Landscape with a Windmill
by Jacob van Ruisdael
Netherlands · 1646 · oil on wood
Christ Preaching ('La Petite Tombe')
by Rembrandt van Rijn
Netherlands · c. 1652 · etching, drypoint, and burin
Banquet of the Crossbowmen's Guild
by Bartholomeus van der Helst
Dutch Golden Age · 1648
The civic guard celebrates the Peace of Münster with a feast — 27 faces, each a recognizable individual. The largest-scale militia portrait of the Golden Age, painted the year the Eighty Years' War ended.
Helena van der Schalcke
by Gerard ter Borch
Dutch Golden Age · c. 1648
A two-year-old in a shimmering white dress, carrying a little basket. Painted at life-size, treated with the gravity usually reserved for adult burghers. Ter Borch's most quietly tender portrait.
Self-Portrait Etching at a Window
by Rembrandt van Rijn
Netherlands · 1648 · Etching
Self-Portrait Drawing at a Window
by Rembrandt van Rijn
Netherlands · 1648 · etching, drypoint, and engraving
The Wheat Field
by Jacob van Ruisdael
Netherlands · 1648 · etching
Self-Portraits by Rembrandt
by Rembrandt
17th century · Etching
Rembrandt made over forty etched self-portraits across his lifetime. The Louvre holds a remarkable set. In ink on paper he is looser and more playful than in his painted selves — pulling faces, squinting, trying on hats.
The Threatened Swan
by Jan Asselijn
Dutch Golden Age · c. 1650
A swan explodes into defense against a dog stalking its nest — wings spread, neck arched, white feathers flying. Later reinterpreted as a political allegory for the Republic defending itself.
The Quack
by Jan Steen
Dutch Golden Age · c. 1650
A traveling mountebank pitches cures to a village crowd, a monkey on his shoulder. Jan Steen painted painters, drunks, taverns, and especially charlatans — the ordinary life he knew best as a tavernkeeper himself.
Landscape with Waterfall
by Jacob van Ruisdael
Dutch Golden Age · c. 1650
A tumbling Scandinavian torrent — even though Ruisdael never left the Low Countries. He drew on Allaert van Everdingen's Norwegian sketches to invent the first Northern European sublime landscape.
Orpheus and the Animals
by Paulus Potter
Dutch Golden Age · 1650
Paulus Potter paints the musician charming lions, leopards, monkeys, and birds into a ring around him — a teenage showpiece for a painter who died at 28, mostly remembered for cows.
Allegory of Winter
by Caesar van Everdingen
Dutch Golden Age · c. 1650
A young woman warms her hands at a brazier, gazing out with a frank smile — a genre scene that doubles as the season. Caesar van Everdingen's clear-lit, classicizing manner was a Haarlem counter to Rembrandt's shadows.
Two Putti Supporting a Torch
by Flemish
Netherlands · 17th Century · 1650–1700 · Terracotta on slate
Fountain of the Four Rivers
by Gian Lorenzo Bernini
Gian Lorenzo Bernini · 1651 · Piazza Navona, Rome
Allegories of the Nile, Ganges, Danube, and Río de la Plata lounge around a hollowed travertine crag supporting an Egyptian obelisk. The Nile covers his face because, according to legend, the source of the river was still unknown — or because he can't bear to look at Borromini's church opposite.
The Ecstasy of Saint Teresa
by Gian Lorenzo Bernini
Gian Lorenzo Bernini · 1652 · Cornaro Chapel, Rome
The Carmelite mystic swoons in midair as a smiling angel lifts a gold-tipped arrow above her heart. Bernini turned the entire chapel into a theater: the Cornaro patrons watch from opera-box side panels, a hidden window rains gilded light down on the marble.
The Virgin and Child with the cat and snake
by Rembrandt van Rijn
Netherlands · 1654
Still Life with a Silver Jug and a Porcelain Bowl
by Willem Kalf
Dutch Golden Age · c. 1655
A Chinese porcelain bowl, a silver ewer, a half-peeled lemon. Willem Kalf's "pronkstilleven" (showy still life) arranges imperial loot from the global trade on one dark table — opulence as quiet drama.
A Bearded Man Wearing a Hat
by Rembrandt van Rijn
Netherlands · c. 1655–60 · oil on canvas
Classical Landscape with Two Women and a Man on a Path
by Attributed to Francisque Millet
France · 17th Century · 1655–1675 · Oil on canvas
Las Meninas
by Diego Velázquez
Spanish Golden Age · Velázquez · 1656
The Infanta and her attendants — but who is the painting for? The king and queen appear only in the mirror. Foucault wrote an essay on that mirror.
Shah Jahan
by Rembrandt van Rijn
Netherlands · c. 1656–61 · pen and brown ink and brush and brown wash
Saint Paul the Hermit
by Mattia Preti
Italy, 17th century · c. 1662–1664 · oil on canvas
St. Francis Beneath a Tree Praying
by Rembrandt van Rijn
Netherlands · 1657 · drypoint and etching
The Little Street
by Johannes Vermeer
Dutch Golden Age · c. 1658
Two brick houses in Delft, mortar visible between every brick. One of only two outdoor scenes Vermeer ever painted. Scholars spent decades trying to identify the exact street.
Trompe-l'Oeil Still Life with a Flower Garland and a Curtain
by Adriaen van der Spelt
Netherlands · 17th Century · 1658 · Oil on panel
The Phoenix or The Statue Overthrown
by Rembrandt van Rijn
Netherlands · 1658 · Etching and drypoint on ivory laid paper
<i>Vanitas</i> Still Life
by Peschier, N. L.
Netherlands · 1659
The Milkmaid
by Johannes Vermeer
Dutch Golden Age · c. 1660
A kitchen maid pours milk into a bowl. Morning light from the window. The whole painting is about the thin stream of milk and the pause it creates.
View of Delft
by Johannes Vermeer
Dutch Golden Age · c. 1660–1661
Vermeer's hometown across the water, clouds moving overhead. Proust called it "the most beautiful painting in the world" and had one of his characters die looking at it.
Festoon of Fruit and Flowers
by Jan Davidsz. de Heem
Dutch Golden Age · 1660
A garland of grapes, peaches, roses, corn, and a lobster hung against a dark niche. Jan Davidsz. de Heem mastered every Flemish still-life tradition and combined them all — the painter's painter of abundance.
Aeneas Receiving Armour from Venus
by Ferdinand Bol
Dutch Golden Age · 1660
Venus, nearly naked, hands her son a golden shield and sword for the coming war. Ferdinand Bol was Rembrandt's pupil; you can feel the master's warm shadow on every fold of drapery.
Silver Wine Jug, Ham, and Fruit
by Abraham van Beyeren
Netherlands · c. 1660–66 · oil on canvas
An Elderly Man in Prayer
by Rembrandt van Rijn
Netherlands · 1660s or later · oil on canvas
Low Waterfall in a Wooded Landscape with a Dead Beech Tree
by Jacob van Ruisdael
Netherlands · c. 1660–70 · oil on canvas
Still Life with Ostrich Egg Cup and the Whitfield Heirlooms
by Pieter Gerritsz. van Roestraeten
Netherlands · 17th Century · 1660–1680 · Oil on canvas
Wooded and Hilly Landscape
by Jacob van Ruisdael
Netherlands · 1660s · oil on canvas
Self-Portrait as the Apostle Paul
by Rembrandt van Rijn
Dutch Golden Age · 1661
Rembrandt at 55, wrapping himself in the turban and sword-hilt of St Paul, gazing out with the exhaustion of his late self-portraits. Painted the year he was forced by bankruptcy to sell his house.
Syndics of the Drapers' Guild
by Rembrandt van Rijn
Dutch Golden Age · 1662
Five cloth-quality inspectors and their servant, caught mid-meeting as if someone had just entered the room. Rembrandt's late group portrait — every figure independently alive, together still a board.
View of Olinda, Brazil
by Frans Post
Dutch colonial · 1662
Frans Post spent seven years with the Dutch West India Company in Brazil. Back in the Netherlands, he painted its ruins — palm trees, armadillos, a distant procession — for the rest of his life. One of the first European painters to depict the tropics firsthand.
The Meeting of Christ with Martha and Mary after the Death of Lazarus
by Rembrandt van Rijn
Netherlands · c. 1662–65 · reed pen and brown ink and wash, heightened with white gouache (in the heads of the kneeling figures)
Woman Reading a Letter
by Johannes Vermeer
Dutch Golden Age · c. 1663
A young woman in blue, pregnant, absorbed in reading. A map of Holland and West Friesland on the wall behind her — the absent correspondent, perhaps at sea. Vermeer at his quietest.
Interior with Women beside a Linen Cupboard
by Pieter de Hooch
Dutch Golden Age · 1663
A mistress hands folded linens to her maid in a tiled hall, sun striking through an open door behind. De Hooch's subject was always the quiet choreography of households — and the light that moved through them.
Wineglass and a Bowl of Fruit
by Willem Kalf
Netherlands · 1663 · oil on canvas
Esther, Ahasuerus, and Haman
by Jan Steen
Netherlands · c. 1668 · oil on canvas
The Fall of the Giants
by Salvator Rosa
Italy · 1663 · Etching with drypoint in black on ivory laid paper
A Watermill
by Meindert Hobbema
Dutch Golden Age · 1664
Hobbema's recurring subject: a watermill at the edge of woods, water churning at its wheel, a figure or two small against the trees. The closing grace note of the Dutch landscape school.
Flowers in a Vase
by Simon Verelst
Netherlands · c. 1669 · oil on wood
Girl with a Pearl Earring
by Johannes Vermeer
Dutch Golden Age · Vermeer · c. 1665
Not a portrait but a "tronie" — a study of an imagined figure. The pearl itself is two quick strokes of white paint. We don't know who she was.
The Jewish Bride
by Rembrandt van Rijn
Dutch Golden Age · c. 1665
A man places his hand on a woman's breast with a tenderness rare in 17th-century painting. Rembrandt laid on the gold of her sleeve with a palette knife; Van Gogh said he would have given ten years of his life to sit in front of it for two weeks.
The Feast of St Nicholas
by Jan Steen
Dutch Golden Age · c. 1665
A Dutch family the morning after December 5th. One daughter clutches a saint's doll and refuses to share; a boy wails because his shoe held only a birch rod. The mother conceals a smile.
The Night School
by Gerrit Dou
Dutch Golden Age · c. 1665
A classroom lit by a single lantern — small faces reading, writing, distracted, bored. Gerrit Dou's specialty was the fijnschilder's tour de force: tiny, almost enamel-smooth, every eyelash visible.
Landscape with a Church by a Torrent
by Jacob van Ruisdael
Netherlands · c. 1670 · oil on canvas
The Art of Painting
by Johannes Vermeer
Dutch Golden Age · c. 1666
Vermeer's studio with a painter from behind, a model posed as Clio the muse of history, and a map of the old Netherlands on the back wall. He kept it until he died — a statement of what he thought his art was for.
The Merry Family
by Jan Steen
Dutch Golden Age · 1668
A household singing, drinking, smoking, children included — above, an inscription: "As the old sing, so pipe the young." A moralizing warning dressed as a party; Steen's signature register.
The Windmill at Wijk bij Duurstede
by Jacob van Ruisdael
Dutch Golden Age · c. 1668
A cylinder-tower mill rises over the Lower Rhine, storm clouds massing behind it. Three women at the water's edge, a boat approaching. Dutch landscape at its weightiest — small figures under an immense sky.
Return of the Prodigal Son
by Rembrandt van Rijn
Baroque · c. 1669
One of the last paintings Rembrandt made. The father's worn hands on the kneeling son's back — one hand rough, one softer, as if both parents — is the emotional weight of a 30-year career distilled into a gesture.
The Love Letter
by Johannes Vermeer
Dutch Golden Age · c. 1669
A mistress is handed a letter by her maid, and looks up from her lute in alarm. We glimpse them through a dark doorway as if intruding. Vermeer plays servant, silence, and thresholds.
The Lacemaker
by Johannes Vermeer
Dutch Golden Age · c. 1669–1671 · Louvre, Paris
Only ten inches tall. A girl bent over her bobbins in concentrated silence — Vermeer's smallest canvas and one of his most intense studies of attention.
Sleeping Christ Child
by Filippo Parodi
Italy · c. 1675 · marble
Communion Cup
England, London · 1671–72 · silver gilt
Still life with Peaches, Red grapes and a Walnut
by Stuven, Ernst
Netherlands · 1675-1712
River Landscape (Koninck)
by Philips Koninck
Dutch Golden Age · 1676
An enormous panorama — a river, fields, cloud shadows, a distant town — all seen from an imaginary elevation the Netherlands cannot provide. Philips Koninck was Rembrandt's best landscape student.
Hunter's Bag on a Terrace
by Melchior d'Hondecoeter
Dutch Golden Age · 1678
A heap of dead hare, birds, and a falcon on a balustrade, gorgeously feathered in death. Hondecoeter was the century's great painter of animals — alive for sport, dead for display.
Palace of Versailles
French Baroque · 1661 onward · France
Louis XIV's hunting lodge turned seat of French monarchy. The Hall of Mirrors reflects the west-facing garden at sunset; every piece of furniture was once pure silver.
Portuguese Synagogue in Amsterdam
by Emanuel de Witte
Dutch Golden Age · 1680
Emanuel de Witte paints the interior of the Esnoga, dogs wandering in the aisle, pulled up with a bare-wood simplicity unusual in church interiors. An image of the most important Sephardic community of the 17th-century world.
Cornelis Evertsen, Lieutenant-Admiral of Zeeland
by Nicolaes Maes
Dutch Golden Age · c. 1680
A weathered naval commander in full armor, painted in the decade of the Dutch Republic's great sea wars against England and France. Nicolaes Maes's late portrait mode at its most confident.
Sabines Crossing the River Tiber to Attend the Roman Games
by After Polidoro Caldara, called Polidoro da Caravaggio
Italy · 1680–1699 · Pen and brown ink
A still-life with a spray of flowers
by Ruysch, Rachel
Netherlands · ca. 1685-1700
Dollhouse of Petronella Oortman
Dutch · c. 1686–1710 · Amsterdam
A cabinet-sized replica of a wealthy Amsterdam house — every miniature porcelain, book, painting, and piece of silver commissioned from real craftsmen. Cost as much as an actual canal house. A Dutch patrician's passion project and one of the greatest dollhouses in the world.
The Elephant, from The Berain Grotesques Series
by After a design by Jean Baptiste Monnoyer
France · 1688–1732 · Wool and silk
Still Life with Flowers in a Glass Vase
by Rachel Ruysch
Dutch Golden Age · 1690
Peonies, roses, tulips, poppies — some past their prime, insects crawling — arranged at impossible angles. Rachel Ruysch worked as a flower painter for 70 years, longer than any of her male contemporaries.
The Régent Diamond
140.64 carats · Galerie d'Apollon
Found in India around 1698, smuggled by a slave who hid it in a wound in his leg, sold to Thomas Pitt (grandfather of William Pitt the Elder), and eventually bought by the French crown. Worn by Louis XV, Louis XVI, and set into Napoleon's sword. Still considered the most perfectly cut historical diamond in the world.
Psyche Presenting the Phial of Water to Venus
by After Raffaello Sanzio, called Raphael
Italy · 1700–1799 · Red chalk
Penitent Magdalene
by After Tiziano Vecellio, called Titian
Italy · 1700–1799 · Red chalk

Pilgrimage to Cythera
by Antoine Watteau
Antoine Watteau · 1717 · Musée du Louvre
Watteau's reception piece for the Académie: couples linger and depart from the island of love, a gilded barge waiting, cupids swirling overhead. The genre he invented — the fête galante — gave eighteenth-century painting its register of wistful pleasure.
Study for The Feast of Love

Italy, 18th century · c. 1726 · pen and brown ink and brush and brown wash over traces of black chalk heightened with lead white
Crown of Louis XV
1722 · Silver gilt, originally set with the crown jewels · Galerie d'Apollon
Made for the coronation of the twelve-year-old Louis XV at Reims. The original diamonds — including the Régent, now displayed nearby — were removed after the ceremony and replaced with paste copies, which are what you see today.

by Meissen porcelain factory
Meissen · ca. 1740-1745
The Marly Horses
by Guillaume Coustou
1739–1745 · Marble · Cour Marly
Commissioned for the royal estate of Marly, where Louis XV wanted rearing horses at the entrance to his watering pond. Each is over 3.5 meters tall. They stood outdoors for nearly two centuries before being brought inside in 1984.

Jean-Honoré Fragonard · 1767 · Wallace Collection
A young woman kicks off her slipper while her hidden lover peeks up her billowing dress — the jilted bishop pushing the swing behind her. The Baron de Saint-Julien commissioned it explicitly to stage this visual joke at his mistress's expense.

Oath of the Horatii
by Jacques-Louis David
Jacques-Louis David · 1784 · Musée du Louvre
Three brothers swear on their father's swords to die for Rome while their women collapse in a curve of grief. Commissioned by Louis XVI and unveiled five years before the Revolution, the painting defined Neoclassical moral rigor for a generation.

The Death of Socrates
by Jacques-Louis David
Jacques-Louis David · 1787 · Metropolitan Museum of Art
Socrates reaches for the hemlock while lecturing his grieving disciples on immortality, one finger stabbing heavenward. David painted it on the eve of the Revolution; Reynolds called it the greatest modern history painting.

Psyche Revived by Cupid's Kiss
by Antonio Canova
Neoclassical · 1793 · Louvre, Paris
Canova's marble of the moment Cupid wakes Psyche with a kiss. Designed to be circled — every angle offers a different composition.
The Death of Marat
by Jacques-Louis David
French Neoclassicism · 1793
Marat in his bathtub, stabbed by Charlotte Corday, pen still in hand. David painted him as a secular martyr — a revolutionary Pietà for a Republic that had killed its king.

Napoleon Crossing the Alps
by Jacques-Louis David
Jacques-Louis David · 1801 · Château de Malmaison
David invented the cinematic pose — rearing horse, windswept cape, outstretched finger — that the actual crossing lacked. Napoleon, who had used a mule, told David to paint him 'calm on a fiery horse' and handed him five replicas to produce.

The Coronation of Napoleon
by Jacques-Louis David
French Neoclassicism · 1805–1807 · Louvre, Paris
Nearly ten meters wide. Napoleon, having just placed the crown on his own head, is about to crown Joséphine. David inserted Napoleon's mother in the grandstand though she refused to attend.

by Possibly after Michelangelo Buonarroti
Italy · 1812–1850 · Pen and brown ink
The Third of May 1808
by Francisco Goya
Spanish Romanticism · 1814
A Madrid citizen, arms flung wide, faces a French firing squad in lantern-light. Painted as an accusation — art reporting on atrocity for the first time.
La Grande Odalisque
by Jean-Auguste-Dominique Ingres
French Neoclassicism · 1814 · Louvre, Paris
A concubine reclines on impossibly long, boneless anatomy. Critics counted extra vertebrae; Ingres was not interested in bones — he was interested in line.

France, 19th century · 1800s · graphite
Leonardo da Vinci
by Raffaello Morghen
Italy, 18th-19th century · 1817 · engraving
Wanderer above the Sea of Fog
by Caspar David Friedrich
German Romanticism · 1818
A man stands on a rocky peak, his back to us, looking into swirling cloud. Romanticism in one image — the sublime, and the lone self against it.
The Raft of the Medusa
by Théodore Géricault
French Romanticism · 1818–1819 · Louvre, Paris
A real recent disaster: 147 men cut loose on a raft off Senegal after the captain took the lifeboats. Fifteen survived. Géricault visited morgues and built a model in his studio.
Portrait of Don Juan Antonio Cuervo
by Francisco de Goya
Spain · 1819 · oil on canvas
Aesacus and Hesperie, plate 66 from Liber Studiorum
by Joseph Mallord William Turner

The Hay Wain
by John Constable
English Romanticism · 1821
A farm cart in a shallow stream on a summer afternoon. Nothing is happening. Constable turned rural quiet into monumental painting.
Saturn Devouring His Son
by Francisco Goya
Black Paintings · 1819–1823
Goya painted it on the wall of his dining room. Old, deaf, sick, he let Saturn eat his son with hollow eyes. Transferred to canvas after his death; not meant for anyone to see.

by Joseph Mallord William Turner

The Milkmaid of Bordeaux
by Francisco Goya
Late Goya · c. 1827
Goya's last canvas, painted at 81 in exile in Bordeaux. A young woman looking down over her shoulder — loose, almost proto-Impressionist brushwork. Bequeathed to his last companion.

Liberty Leading the People
by Eugène Delacroix
French Romanticism · 1830 · Louvre, Paris
Painted after the July Revolution that overthrew Charles X. Liberty strides over the barricades, bare-breasted, bayonet and tricolor raised. The boy with pistols later became Hugo's Gavroche.

The Gleaners
by Jean-François Millet
Realism · 1857
Three peasant women stoop to pick up leftover grain after the harvest. The wealthy hated it — it looked like socialism. Millet painted rural labor as dignified, not picturesque.

The Napoleon III Apartments
1861 · Richelieu Wing
Not a single object but an entire suite of state rooms — crimson silk, gilded stucco, chandeliers heavy enough to frighten the ceiling. These were the ceremonial apartments of the Minister of State under the Second Empire. Walk in and the museum disappears.

Impression, Sunrise
Impressionism · Monet · 1872 · Le Havre
The painting that gave a movement its name — a hostile critic meant "impression" as an insult. Hazy harbor, red sun, no detail that isn't a brushstroke.

Dance at Le Moulin de la Galette
by Pierre-Auguste Renoir
Pierre-Auguste Renoir · 1876 · Musée d'Orsay
A Sunday afternoon at the Montmartre dance hall, sunlight filtering through acacia leaves onto the dancers in dapples Renoir invented a new vocabulary of color to paint. His friends posed for it over several months.

Luncheon of the Boating Party
by Pierre-Auguste Renoir
Pierre-Auguste Renoir · 1881 · The Phillips Collection
Renoir's friends — among them his future wife Aline, caressing a little dog — laze on a restaurant terrace overlooking the Seine. It is impressionism at its most convivial: no protest, no weather, just a warm afternoon extended indefinitely.

A Sunday on La Grande Jatte
by Georges Seurat
Post-Impressionism · Seurat · 1884–1886
Pointillism's founding canvas — ten feet wide, built entirely from tiny dots. Parisians on a Sunday afternoon, rendered like nothing before.
The Potato Eaters
by Vincent van Gogh
Early Van Gogh · 1885 · Nuenen
Dark, muddy, deliberately ugly. Van Gogh wrote that he wanted to show hands that had dug the earth eating the potatoes those hands had grown. His breakthrough, though critics hated it.

Self-Portrait (Van Gogh)
by Vincent van Gogh
Post-Impressionism · 1887
A Paris self-portrait, jacket gray-green, hat a pale block. Van Gogh painted 35+ self-portraits across his career; this one marks his turn toward the dotted Impressionist brushwork he would soon abandon for thicker, louder color.

Sunflowers
by Vincent van Gogh
Post-Impressionism · 1888
Painted in Arles to decorate Gauguin's guest bedroom. Van Gogh made seven versions; in letters, he called them an ode to gratitude.
Café Terrace at Night
by Vincent van Gogh
Post-Impressionism · 1888 · Arles
The yellow terrace still stands in Arles (renamed after the painting). No black in the night sky — deep cobalt blue everywhere instead. Van Gogh's first major work done at night.
The Bedroom
by Vincent van Gogh
Post-Impressionism · 1888
Van Gogh's own room at the Yellow House in Arles — painted flat, walls tilted, colors simplified to "rest the mind." Three versions survive; he made the second from memory in a mental hospital.

by Claude Monet

by Morisot, Berthe
France · 1888
The Starry Night
by Vincent van Gogh
Post-Impressionism · Van Gogh · 1889
Painted from the window of his asylum room at Saint-Rémy, from memory. The village below is imagined; the swirling sky he said he could actually see.
The Burghers of Calais
by Auguste Rodin
Modern sculpture · 1889
Six townsmen walking out to surrender themselves to Edward III to save their city. Rodin rejected the usual heroic pedestal — they stand at ground level, each alone with his own weight.

Servant Girls on the Leidsegracht
by Isaac Israels
Amsterdam Impressionism · c. 1890
Two maids pause on an Amsterdam canal bridge, a blur of low-light city behind them. Isaac Israels painted the working city when official Dutch taste still preferred prettier subjects.

The Thinker
by Auguste Rodin

Originally a small figure above The Gates of Hell — Dante, looking down at his Inferno. Enlarged and cast on its own, it became a universal emblem of thought.

The Scream
by Edvard Munch
Expressionism · Edvard Munch · 1893
Munch wrote in his diary: "I sensed an infinite scream passing through nature." The sky's red may be from the Krakatoa eruption ten years earlier.

The Sleeping Gypsy
by Henri Rousseau
Henri Rousseau · 1897 · Museum of Modern Art
A dark-robed traveler sleeps beside her mandolin while a lion sniffs her face under a flat full moon. Rousseau, a self-taught customs clerk, offered to sell it to his hometown mayor for 200 francs; he was politely declined.

Les Demoiselles d'Avignon
by Pablo Picasso
Proto-Cubism · 1907
Five Barcelona prostitutes, faces half-African mask, bodies fractured into angular planes. Picasso hid it for years; when it emerged it had already ended the 19th century.

Norway · 1908–9 · lithograph
Alpha and Omega: The Wife Omega and the Donkey
by Edvard Munch
Norway · 1908–9 · lithograph
Café Wepler
by Édouard Vuillard
France, late 19th-early 20th Century · c. 1908–10, reworked in 1912 · oil on fabric
Fight Between a Tiger and a Buffalo
by Henri Rousseau
France, late 19th-early 20th Century · 1908 · oil on fabric
Bangle
by August Hollming
St Petersburg (Russia) · 1908-1913
Frogs and Lily Pads (Grenouilles et Nénuphars) Vase
by René Lalique
France, 20th century · 1909–12 · glass
Houses at Murnau
by Vasily Kandinsky
Germany · 1909 · Oil on cardboard
Wrestlers in a Circus
by Ernst Ludwig Kirchner
Germany, 20th century · 1909 · oil on canvas
The Dance
by Henri Matisse
Fauvism · 1909–1910
Five red figures hold hands in a frenzied ring against blue sky and green earth. Matisse called the composition "the highest point of the story" — the first great Modernist painting of pure color.
The Dream (Rousseau)
by Henri Rousseau
Henri Rousseau · 1910 · Museum of Modern Art
Rousseau's final completed work: a nude Yadwigha reclines on a Second Empire sofa that floats through a moonlit jungle of invented foliage, a snake charmer piping behind. He insisted the sofa's incongruity was perfectly explained by the word 'dream.'
Notre Dame
by Paul Signac
France · 1910 · Watercolor
Seated Woman from the Front with Hat, Face Hooded
by Gustav Klimt
Austria · 1910 · Graphite
Sturgeon and case
by Faberge
Russia · ca.1910
Kangaroo and case
by Faberge
Russia · ca.1910
Hissing baboon and case
by Faberge
Russia · ca. 1910
Chinchilla
by Faberge
Russia · ca.1910
Water Lilies
by Claude Monet
Impressionism · Monet · 1896–1926 · Giverny
Two hundred and fifty paintings of the pond in his garden, late in life, eyes failing. The largest canvases wrap around the room at the Orangerie.
I and the Village
by Marc Chagall
Cubist / Expressionist · 1911
A green-faced peasant and a white goat exchange a look across a village that floats and spins. Chagall's memory of his hometown of Vitebsk, filtered through Paris.
The Red Studio
by Henri Matisse
Henri Matisse · 1911 · Museum of Modern Art
Matisse soaked his Issy studio in uniform Venetian red, leaving his own paintings as the only color notes on the wall. He had begun with green and blue underneath; the radical decision to cover everything in a single pigment recast what a painting could be.
Nude Man with Raised Arms (recto) Seated Man (verso)
by Egon Schiele
Austria · 1911 · graphite

by John Singer Sargent
America · 1911 · watercolor with traces of graphite

by Vasily Kandinsky

by Piet Mondrian
Netherlands · 1911–1921 · Oil on canvas

by House of Fabergé

Seated female nude
by Schmidt-Rottluff, Karl
Germany · 1912
Composition VII
by Wassily Kandinsky
Abstract · 1913
Kandinsky called it the most complex work he ever painted — over thirty preparatory studies. Motifs of resurrection, flood, and last judgment dissolve into color and line.

by Vasily Kandinsky
Germany · Modernism · 1913 · Oil on canvas

Marigold
by William Morris
England, 20th century · 20th century · plain weave cotton, printed
The Elephant Celebes
by Max Ernst
Max Ernst · 1921 · Tate Modern
A giant metallic elephant based on a photograph of a Sudanese corn bin looms over a headless mannequin beside a mysterious fish-stalk. Ernst's breakthrough Surrealist work, painted before André Breton had even coined the term.
Woman before an Aquarium
by Henri Matisse
France · 1921–1923 · Oil on canvas
Woman on Rose Divan
by Henri Matisse
France · 1921 · Oil on canvas
Lozenge Composition with Yellow, Black, Blue, Red, and Gray
by Piet Mondrian
Netherlands · Modernism · 1921 · Oil on canvas
Senecio
by Paul Klee
Paul Klee · 1922 · Kunstmuseum Basel
A geometrically segmented face painted in ochres and pinks, the eyes slightly mismatched so it never quite looks back. Klee claimed the title 'Old Man' (Senecio in Latin); the image belongs somewhere between clown, sun god, and child's drawing.
Das graphische Werk von Egon Schiele
by Schiele
Austria · 1922
The Witch and the Comb
by Klee
Germany · 1922

by Paul Klee
Germany, 20th century · 1923 · color lithograph

by Paul Klee

Ships near the Trieux River

by Breuer, Marcel Lajos

Paris, Le Pont des Arts avec Remorqeurs

Composition with Red, Yellow, and Blue
by Piet Mondrian
Netherlands · 1927 · oil on canvas
Singing Man
by Ernst Barlach
Germany, 20th century · 1928 · bronze
The Treachery of Images
by René Magritte
Surrealism · 1929
A pipe painted with the words "Ceci n'est pas une pipe" (This is not a pipe). It isn't — it's a painting of one. Magritte's founding joke of Surrealism.
Lighthouse Village (also known as Cape Elizabeth)
by Edward Hopper
America · 1929 · watercolor with gouache over graphite
The Calculating Old Man
by Paul Klee
Germany, 20th century · 1929 · etching
Composition with Red, Blue and Yellow
by Piet Mondrian
De Stijl · 1930
Black lines, three primary colors, white negative space, canvas flat as the grid itself. Mondrian believed he had found the underlying logic of visible reality.
American Gothic
by Grant Wood
Grant Wood · 1930 · Art Institute of Chicago
Wood's sister Nan and his dentist Byron McKeeby posed as a farmer and his daughter in front of a white-board Iowa house. Meant by Wood as a gentle tribute, the image has been parodied perhaps more than any other American painting.
The Persistence of Memory
by Salvador Dalí
Surrealism · 1931
Four clocks melting on a Catalan beach, an eyelashed creature asleep. Dalí said the watches came from Camembert in the sun. An image that somehow filed itself directly into the collective mind.
The Worship of the Golden Calf
by Chagall, Marc
France · 1931-1939
Crouching Youth
by Turner, Winifred
England · ca. 1934
Composition (No. 1) Gray-Red
by Piet Mondrian
Netherlands · 1935 · Oil on canvas
Mother and Child
by Turner, Alfred
England · ca. 1936
Strawberry Thief
by William Morris
England, Merton Abbey, 20th century · c 1936 · plain weave cotton, discharge printed
God of War
by Paul Klee
Germany, 20th century · 1937 · Gouache and tempera
Study for a Composition
by Piet Mondrian
Netherlands · 1940–1941 · Collage of cut and pasted papers
Nighthawks
by Edward Hopper
Edward Hopper · 1942 · Art Institute of Chicago
Four figures in a late-night Greenwich Avenue diner, insulated by its curved glass from the empty street outside. Painted weeks after Pearl Harbor, it fixed the look of twentieth-century American loneliness.
Broadway Boogie Woogie
by Piet Mondrian
Piet Mondrian · 1943 · Museum of Modern Art
Mondrian's last completed painting, made after he fled wartime Europe for New York. The jittering yellow grid mimics Manhattan's street plan and the jazz he had fallen in love with; black bars are banished for the first time in decades.
Perseus's Last Duty
by Max Beckmann
Germany, 20th century · 1949 · oil on canvas
Cavalier sur sa Monture
by Picasso, Pablo
